text: Set up my Echo Meter from 18:45-23:17.
It was triggered 80 times with every call being Tadarida brasiliensis besides one Perimyotis subflavus call at 20:23.

I uploaded three of the TADBRA calls (one of the earliest calls at 19:17, one with a feeding buzz at 20:42 and the last call I recorded at 23:16 High frequency recording made with Echo Meter Touch 2 Pro microphone connected to old Fire tablet.

With Valeri Ponzo, Brenda Jerry Callaway, and two others: a monumentally successful visit to see the Little Bunting, a crazily fantastically epic little bird -- the first for Florida and the first in North America east of Arizona and northwestern Mexico.
The landowner found the bunting on 1 Mar and posted photographs to ABA s What s That Bird website, where it was eventually identified as a Little Bunting.
The landowner publicly requested no visitors but privately invited selected birders to visit.
Val and I were two of the lucky 170 birders selected to visit.
We missed the bunting on 12 Mar but returned this day and saw it for 7-8 glorious minutes from maybe 20 feet away.
The weather at the start was partly sunny and 46 degrees ( feels like 41), with a north wind (15-20 MPH elsewhere but pretty calm in the yard).
We left at 0845 CST and headed to Bayview Park for more Escambia County birding.
Eastern Gray Squirrel (Sciurus carolinensis) observed at Florida, Escambia, Pensacola, Semur Road.
With Valeri Ponzo, Fran Meyerson, Brenda Jerry Callaway, and others; a horribly painful, unsuccessful visit to see the Little Bunting.
Val and Fran left Sarasota around 2240 Saturday night and they met me at Interstate 75 and State Road 50 in Hernando County at 0030 Sunday morning.
Fran drove all the way to Pensacola, arriving at 0710 CST (during the time change to DST).
After waiting at Pintado Park, we headed to the yard when we heard that the bunting was present (we were scheduled as the 0800 crew).
We were at the yard within a few minutes but the bunting had flown.
We stayed for 6.5 hours but the bunting did not return.
We left at 1405 CST so that Val and I could be at work Monday morning.
It was a long, hard drive back...
The weather at the start was overcast and 73 degrees with winds from the SW at 12 MPH.
The weather probably affected the bunting s behavior.
Val and I returned on Saturday, 18 Mar and got magnificent views of the bunting, but Fran did not return.
Eastern Gray Squirrel (Sciurus carolinensis) observed at Florida, Escambia, Pensacola, Semur Road.

An unexpected trip south from a job in Lakeland via meeting the crew in Tampa at 0530.
Val texted me that the Black-billed Cuckoo found yesterday at Pinecraft Park in Sarasota was seen again this morning, and she was going for it when she got off work at noon.
She found the cuckoo -- a lifer! -- by 1315 and called me.
I headed down from Tampa and Val showed me the cuckoo, which was extremely cooperative, almost immediately.
It was a photographic lifer for me; Black-billed Cuckoo is a surprisingly tough bird in Florida; I ve seen only three or four in 40 years of birding the state.
The weather at the start was sunny, 83 degrees ( feels like 87), and breezy.
We left at 1558 and headed to Gecko s Grill for dinner.
Because I had no optics with me, I used Val s camera to take these images.
Val s camera tends to take slightly unfocused photographs more often than focused photographs, for some inexplicable reason.
Plus, the lighting was pretty dreadful, so many of my images are intentionally overexposed to show detail in the subjects.

A solo visit after work; a successful chase of the Lesser Goldfinch, the second Florida record (the first was 15-18 July 2014)., and of the western green-backed race.
I left work in Zephyrhills at 1315 and arrived at the goldfinch yard at 1459.
The weather at the start was overcast and 87 degrees ( feels like 92), with calm winds.
The goldfinch appeared, silently, at 1618 and sat still in a Laurel Oak for a minutes or two.
The it flew to the feeder for 10 seconds, then flew to a Camphortree, where it sat for 3 minutes.
I remained and chatted with Tami for more than an hour, watching the feeders somewhat intently, then left at 1805.

text: This was the first of three raccoons that we observed walking the dike on the west shore of Lake Hancock in Circle B Bar Reserve, Lakeland, Polk County, FL.
Each raccoon ambled along the pathway and payed almost no attention to us as it searched for food.
We did observe one raccoon feeding on Florida Softshell Turtle eggs while the two turtles laying side by side were laying their eggs.
Thus, we came to the conclusion that the raccoons behavior was to forage at the time of day the turtles were laying their eggs.
Since they were in a protected reserve, they showed no fear of humans, but they did move to the other side of the 6-foot wide path to pass by us.
Common Raccoon (Procyon lotor) observed at Polk County, FL, USA.
This Raccoon waddled right beside us on the dike pathway then disappeared off the dike.
When we caught up to it, it was feeding on Florida softshell turtle (Apalone ferox) eggs - WHILE the female was laying them!
On examining our photos, we realized there were TWO turtles (count the rounded mounds) and the raccoon was obtaining eggs from both (turtle 1 in photos 2, 4 and 5; turtle 2 in photos 3 and 6).
The 7th photo is a screen shot of the raccoon and turtles from a video.
It shows a second raccoon that came down the dike and attempted to share the feast.
The first raccoon charged and growled at it and the second wandered away hungry.
Common Raccoon (Procyon lotor) observed at Polk County, FL, USA.

text: The eastern gray squirrel is a tree squirrel native to eastern North America, where it is the natural cultivator of forests and ecologically essential.
Widely introduced to various locations around the world, the eastern gray squirrel in Europe is considered an invasive species.

It has been included since 2016 in the list of invasive species of concern to the EU so it cannot be imported, raised, transported, traded, or intentionally released into the environment throughout the European Union, and in some countries have even displaced the native red squirrels.

NUMBER: 20231012 SPECIES: Evening Bat, Nycticeius humeralis Rafinesque, 1818 DISTINGUISHING: Small bat with brown body fur, black ears, and no fur on muzzle.
COLLECTION: EHW was carrying 2-day fallen dead palm fronds when he felt something trying to bite him.
Having been bitten by almost everything on the land and in the sea, he recognized the attempt.
He threw down the fronds and took a collection plastic bag from his pocket, found, captured, and photographed a bat.
The specimen was held until dark to keep the released bat from day-time predation.
It was placed in a bush at a 2 m elevation adjacent to the capture locality.
All bats in Florida are protected.
DATE, TIME: 12 October 2023, 3:14 PM LOCALITY: 920 St.
Andrews, Naples, Florida 34113 LONGITUDE, LATITUDE: 26.10716, 81.71515 DISTRIBUTION: Eastern and Midwest USA, NE Mexico.
Has recently spread further north, either replacing other species killed by White-nose Disease, or due to Global Warming.
Common in Florida except in the Keys.
ROOSTING: This species roosts in groups of a few to 100 specimens.
Sometimes other species are included in a roost.
Our specimen was in a dead palm frown on the ground.
It cannot take off in flight from the ground.
Why it remained 2 days on the ground is unclear.
It did not appear to be injured.
SIMILAR SPECIES: Resemble Big Brown Bats, Eptesicus fuscus (Beauvois, 1796), but are much smaller.
And also uncommon in south Florida.
DANGEROUS: Bats have an undeserved reputation for frequently infecting humans with rabies.
This very rarely occurs.
Our rabid Racoon (Williams and Bunkley-Williams, 2019) was far more dangerous.
IMPORANTANCE: Unusual roosting and remaining on ground.
Williams, Jr. 2,3,5,6 and Dr.
Lucy Bunkley-Williams 2,4,5,7 REFERENCES: Anonymous. 2023.

text: Class: Mammalia- Had fur around its face indicating that it is a mammal.
Subclass: Theria- Is not a Monotreme since it had teeth and was found in North America where there are no Monotreme species.
Infraclass: Placentalia- The armadillo did not have a marsupium pouch.
Order: Xenarthra- the armadillo had its body covered in cornified scales that were underlain with osteoderm, which is indicative of some members of Xenarthra and is not present in any other Orders.
It is not in Pholidota since pangolins are found in Africa and Asia and not in North America.
Furthermore, the scales were not made of agglutinated hair and did not match the pattern of scales found in pangolins.
The picture of the armadillo was taken after it had rained earlier in the day resulting in a slightly cold temperature.
The armadillo was present in the leaf litter of a wooded area and stayed within brushes on the side of the trail.
At the time of the photo, the armadillo was startled and moved away into the dense brush alongside the trail away from me.

Manatees are large, aquatic mammals known for their gentle nature and unique appearance.
Belonging to the order Sirenia, they inhabit warm coastal waters, estuaries, and freshwater rivers.
Manatees are characterized by their streamlined bodies, paddle-like flippers, and rounded tails.
They are herbivores, feeding on aquatic plants.
Manatees are often referred to as sea cows and are primarily found in the waters of Florida, the Caribbean, and parts of West Africa.
Unfortunately, they face threats such as boat collisions and habitat loss, leading to their classification as vulnerable or endangered species.
Conservation efforts aim to protect these gentle giants and their habitats.
West Indian Manatee (Trichechus manatus) observed at Monroe County, US-FL, US.
The Key deer (Odocoileus virginianus clavium) is a subspecies of the white-tailed deer native to the Florida Keys in the United States.
Characterized by its smaller size compared to mainland white-tailed deer, the Key deer is considered an endangered species.
These deer inhabit the tropical hardwood hammocks and pine rocklands of the Florida Keys.
Conservation efforts have been implemented to protect the Key deer population, including habitat preservation and wildlife management measures.
These efforts aim to ensure the survival of this unique and iconic species in its limited and specific habitat.
White-tailed Deer (Odocoileus virginianus) observed at Orange County, FL, USA.
Oceanic dolphins are a diverse group of marine mammals belonging to the family Delphinidae.
Known for their intelligence, social behavior, and acrobatic displays, dolphins inhabit oceans and seas worldwide.
They are characterized by a streamlined body, a dorsal fin, and a highly developed brain.
Dolphins are carnivorous, feeding on fish and squid, and are known for their sophisticated communication using clicks, whistles, and body language.
Common Bottlenose Dolphin (Tursiops truncatus) observed at Florida keys.
Common Pipistrelles are often found in a variety of habitats, including urban areas, woodlands, and meadows.
They feed primarily on moths, flies, and other small insects, making them beneficial for pest control.
During the day, they roost in trees, crevices, or buildings.
These bats play a crucial role in maintaining ecological balance by controlling insect populations.
However, like many bat species, they face threats such as habitat loss, pesticide use, and disturbances to roosting sites, making conservation efforts important for their well-being.

The coyote was attempting to cross the road at the time of observation.
The coyote was approximately 4 feet long and 2 feet tall.
It was a tannish grey color with a darker brown coat covering the upper head, back, and tail region.
Due to its size and lack of observable genital s the assumption is that it is female.
The small size, pointy snout, and poofy tail were additional indicators that the organism was a Coyote.
While the coyote was seen on the road it most likely lived in the forest area across the street.
Coyotes are known to be adaptable to many environments and even urban areas but they primarily live in forests and plains.

text: This is a deer, personally one of my favorite wildlife animals, they have very soft brown colored skin fur with pointed out ears.
Meanwhile they may look normal sized, they are actually very heavy wildlife animals.
These deer have very strong muscles and may damage cars when colliding.
The deer is in the family cervidae and its defining characteristic would be their body structure and their pointed out ears.
Their strong muscles keep them alive and moving at all times.
Description reference: I found this dear in a forest right by my community.
Known geographic distribution: they are native to all continents except Australia and Antarctica.

text: A visit after work; only my second visit here -- the first was years ago -- to see if I could find any butterflies or moths.
I walked around the southern boundary all the way to Cliff Stephens Park, then walked the northern boundary back to Jet.

There were TONS of small moths, caterpillars, and a pupae in oaks and other hardwood shrubs and trees along the northern boundary, while the southern boundary had some blooming (Joe) Bidens that attracted several butterflies.
I tried to photograph every moth food plant in case some species are not known to be moth hosts, as well as to hopefully narrow down the moth identification possibilities.
Moths were so abundant that walking by nearly every low-growing oak meant that 4, 5, 6, or more moths would fly out.
Some landed on my shirt, pants, or hand!
I also found a super-cool leafhopper that hopefully can be identified to species.
The park seemed to include centuries-old pines (I think Longleaf Pine but I m not positive) and Live Oaks; I was not expecting either.
As this is an urban park, there were many exotic plants growing in it.
However, I believe that none were intentionally planted -- rather, they naturalized -- so that all plants should be Research-Gradable.
I used my Panasonic Lumix FZ80 for all non-macro photographs, and I used my Samsung A52 cell phone for macro-photography.
The weather at my arrival was sunny and 73 degrees ( feels like 73) with a light breeze.
I left around 1635 and battled traffic all the way home.
I am submitting 180 records from Kapok Park.
Eastern Gray Squirrel (Sciurus carolinensis) observed at Florida, Pinellas, Clearwater, Kapok Park.
A visit after work; only my second visit here -- the first was years ago -- to see if I could find any butterflies or moths.
I walked around the southern boundary all the way to Cliff Stephens Park, then walked the northern boundary back to Jet.

There were TONS of small moths, caterpillars, and a pupae in oaks and other hardwood shrubs and trees along the northern boundary, while the southern boundary had some blooming (Joe) Bidens that attracted several butterflies.
I tried to photograph every moth food plant in case some species are not known to be moth hosts, as well as to hopefully narrow down the moth identification possibilities.
Moths were so abundant that walking by nearly every low-growing oak meant that 4, 5, 6, or more moths would fly out.
Some landed on my shirt, pants, or hand!
I also found a super-cool leafhopper that hopefully can be identified to species.
The park seemed to include centuries-old pines (I think Longleaf Pine but I m not positive) and Live Oaks; I was not expecting either.
As this is an urban park, there were many exotic plants growing in it.
However, I believe that none were intentionally planted -- rather, they naturalized -- so that all plants should be Research-Gradable.
I used my Panasonic Lumix FZ80 for all non-macro photographs, and I used my Samsung A52 cell phone for macro-photography.
The weather at my arrival was sunny and 73 degrees ( feels like 73) with a light breeze.
I left around 1635 and battled traffic all the way home.

text: Adult and calf (?) foraging on grass on the edge of a canal Florida Manatee (Trichechus manatus latirostris) observed at Miami-Dade County, US-FL, US.
This one is an insane story.
I got a tip that a male sperm whale had beached down in Venice Beach FL, just about an hour south of where I live.
Took the chance with two of my friends and we ended up staying the night.
We were essentially the only ones that saw him pass away.
Once the officials started gathering, (MOTE, CMA, FFAW, etc.) we heard that they had no idea what happened during the night.
We were able to inform them and once they found out how long we had stayed they asked if we wanted to help out with the necropsy!
One of the most beautiful animals I ve ever seen.
We also got to fill out a Mortality report, fully documenting the events that happened during the night, including video, illustrations, and images.
The three of us named him Orion due to the constellation that was above him while he was still alive.
The final statistics was that he was a male individual, that was 44 feet long, and weight approximately 70,000 pounds.
He was also underweight, most likely due to the still as of yet undetermined cause of death.
Sperm Whale (Physeter macrocephalus) observed at Florida, US.

I was here after hours with permission and had UV blacklights running from 10PM to sunrise to attract insects (started photographing them at midnight).
Full moon and windy conditions limited how many insects I got (there were almost no mosquitoes, a bad sign when it comes to blacklighting).
See link at the bottom for details about my various set ups for the night and my 2024 CNC in general.

At 8:31 pm, May 24, we were driving slowly on the main park road of Everglades NP road cruising for snakes when Riley and I both clearly saw a medium sized mammal step off the road and into the grass.
It was absolutely a cat, far larger than a house cat but slightly smaller than the panther statue at the Everglades visitor center.
It was tan with a long tail.
It was already going in the bushes while we realized what we were seeing.
It briefly looked at us when I shakily took the worst iPhone photo of my life, and then disappeared into the bushes.
It didn t run or move particularly fast the whole time- no wonder they get hit by cars.
It didn t seem to be collared.
I wrote this right after we saw it as proof I am not a crazy person.
